1: Psalm 71 is an anonymous psalm widely understood to be an old man's prayer based on Psalms 71:9, 18 ('when old age comes,' 'in old age and gray hair'). It is heavily intertextual, weaving together language from Ps 22 (birth-covenant), Ps 31 (rock of refuge), and Ps 22's pattern of lament to praise. 'In you, O LORD, I take refuge; let me never be put to shame' — the opening line quotes Psalms 31:1 nearly verbatim, establishing the psalm as a consciously composed meditation on earlier psalmic tradition. The psalmist is one who has been formed by the liturgical texts of the community and prays out of that formation.
2: 'In your righteousness deliver me and rescue me; incline your ear to me and save me' — the appeal to YHWH's covenant righteousness as the basis for deliverance. YHWH's tsedaqah (righteousness) was not merely a moral category but a relational-covenantal one: the committed faithfulness to his people that constitutes his covenant character. To appeal to YHWH's righteousness is to appeal to what YHWH owes to himself and to the covenant relationship. 'Incline your ear' is the posture of the attentive patron bending toward the client in court.
3: 'Be for me a sheltering rock, a place I can always come to; you have commanded my salvation, for you are my rock and my fortress' — the repeated rock-and-fortress metaphor throughout Book II (Ps 42-72). The specific phrase 'a place I can always come to' implies the psalmist's ongoing return to this refuge — not a one-time rescue but a habitual shelter sought throughout life. 'You have commanded my salvation' is a striking claim: YHWH has decreed the psalmist's salvation as a sovereign act.
4: 'Rescue me, O my God, from the hand of the wicked, from the grip of the unjust and cruel' — 'the unjust and cruel' are those who use power without covenant restraint. The 'hand' and 'grip' of the wicked describe the social mechanism of oppression: being physically or legally controlled by those with power. Ancient Israel's legal system gave significant power to creditors, landowners, and patrons; without YHWH's intervention, the powerless person had no recourse against those who could grip them.
5: 'For you, O Lord GOD, are my hope, my trust from my earliest years' — 'Lord GOD' (Adonai Yhwh) is the doubled divine title that appears in formal covenant address and prophetic literature (Ezek uses it frequently; Genesis 15:2, 8: Abraham's prayer). 'My hope from my earliest years' — the lifelong orientation of trust toward YHWH from youth: the psalmist's entire existence has been structured by covenant dependence, not as a late resort but as the original condition of life.
6: 'I have depended on you from before I was born; you drew me safely out of my mother's womb. My praise is forever your own' — the birth-covenant language echoes Psalms 22:9-10 and Isaiah 49:1 (the Servant formed in the womb). Prenatal dependence on YHWH is the most extreme statement of total covenant reliance: even before consciousness or choice, the psalmist's existence was sustained by divine care. YHWH present at the physical moment of birth as active helper established the covenant relationship before any human decision was possible.
7: 'I have been like an astonishing sign to many, but you are my strong shelter' — 'an astonishing sign (mofet, wonder/portent) to many' — the psalmist's life has been visible to others as a remarkable thing: perhaps repeated rescues, long life, or survival through adversity that made others take notice. Mofet is often used for miraculous signs given by prophets (Exodus 7:9; Deuteronomy 13:1-3; Isaiah 8:18: 'I and the children the LORD has given me are signs and wonders in Israel'). The psalmist's survival is itself a visible testimony to YHWH's sustaining power.
8: 'My mouth is full of your praise and your glory all the day long' — praise as the continuous occupation of the psalmist's mouth: the expression of thankfulness that cannot wait for special occasions but fills daily speech. 'Glory' (tipharetecha, your splendor/beauty) is YHWH's covenant glory as the content of constant proclamation. The mouth filled with praise replaces the mouth that might be filled with complaint or fear — the discipline of praise as covenant orientation even amid the crisis described in Psalms 71:9-12.
9: 'Do not reject me when old age comes; do not abandon me when my strength gives out' — the distinctive petition of this psalm: the fear specific to old age. In ancient Israel, when a person's physical strength declined, their social value in the patron-client system could decrease proportionally. The fear is that YHWH might relate to the psalmist as human patrons do — withdrawing as the client becomes unable to contribute. The petition asserts that the divine patron does not operate on the social contract of mutual benefit.
10: 'For my enemies speak about me; those who watch for my life conspire together' — the enemies taking advantage of the elderly psalmist's vulnerability. Those who 'watch for my life' are the scouts who monitor the target, waiting for the moment to strike. Conspiracy against the vulnerable elder follows the pattern of Ps 31 and 41 — the council of enemies working in concert against the isolated covenant sufferer. Elderly or infirm people in ancient Near Eastern societies were particularly vulnerable to such exploitation.
11: 'They say, God has abandoned him; run him down and take him, for there is no one to rescue him' — the 'God has abandoned him' taunt is the enemies' theological argument: the psalmist's vulnerability in old age is read as evidence of divine abandonment. The same logic appears in Psalms 22:8; Psalms 41:11 — suffering is interpreted as divine withdrawal. 'There is no one to rescue him' — the patronage network has collapsed; no human patron remains. This makes YHWH the last and only recourse — which is precisely where the psalmist wants to press the petition.
12: 'O God, do not be far from me; O my God, come quickly to my aid' — the spatial urgency: YHWH's distance (or perceived distance) is the crisis. The 'come quickly' has specific military resonance: the reinforcements arriving before the position falls. Psalms 22:19 uses the same language. The elderly psalmist's sense of being surrounded and the urgency of the petition mirror the Passion Psalm's structure.
13: 'Let those who attack my life be put to shame and consumed; let those who seek my harm be wrapped in reproach and disgrace' — the imprecatory petition: the reversal of shame onto those who sought to inflict it. In the honor-shame world, the prayer for enemies to be 'put to shame' was a legal appeal to the divine judge to render a verdict against the accusers. The language of total social humiliation returns upon those who sought it for the psalmist.
14: 'But I will hope continually and will praise you more and more' — the deliberate contrast with Psalms 71:13's petition against enemies: the psalmist's own path is not revenge or bitterness but escalating hope and praise. 'More and more' (I will add to all your praises) — praise as an accumulating practice: each act of praise adds to the corpus of the psalmist's testimony. The aging psalmist does not diminish in covenant engagement but amplifies it.
15: 'My mouth will proclaim your righteousness and your saving acts all the day long, for I cannot count their number' — the uncountable nature of YHWH's saving acts: the psalmist's lifetime of experience with divine rescue has produced more incidents than can be enumerated. The accumulation of YHWH's faithfulness through the years exceeds the capacity of memory and speech to catalog. The old man's prayer is precisely that he has too many rescues to list, not too few.
16: 'I will come in the strength of the Lord GOD; I will declare your righteousness — yours and no one else's' — 'in the strength of the Lord GOD' (bigvurot Adonai Yhwh) — YHWH's power is the basis for the psalmist's approach to the sanctuary. The double divine title signals formal covenant address. 'Yours and no one else's' (tsidqatecha levadecha, your righteousness alone) — the emphatic exclusivity: YHWH's righteousness is the only righteousness that has sustained the psalmist, not the psalmist's own merit or human assistance.
17: 'O God, you have taught me from my youth, and to this day I am still declaring your wonderful deeds' — the intergenerational covenant education: YHWH as the divine teacher from youth (cf. Psalms 25:4-5, 8-9; Proverbs 22:6). The psalmist's lifelong testimony is the fruit of this lifelong divine instruction. The connection between being taught and being a teacher/testifier is direct: YHWH's formation produces the psalmist's proclamation.
18: 'And even now in old age, with gray hair, O God, do not forsake me until I have proclaimed your strength to this generation' — the explicit generational mission: the elderly psalmist wants to live long enough to transmit YHWH's covenant acts to the next generation. The Deuteronomic transmission command (Deuteronomy 6:20-25; Psalms 78:4-7) required the parents' generation to pass on YHWH's story. The survival of the old person's proclamation task is the stated reason for continued life.
19: 'Your righteousness, O God, reaches the high heavens; you who have done such great things, O God — who is like you?' — the incomparability formula (mi kamocha, who is like you?) is the standard rhetorical form for asserting YHWH's uniqueness among the gods (Exodus 15:11; Psalms 35:10; Psalms 86:8; Psalms 89:6, 8; Isaiah 40:18, 25; Psalms 46:5). YHWH's righteousness 'reaching the high heavens' establishes the cosmic scope of a moral attribute that other ANE religions attached to physical power or territory — YHWH's righteousness is as expansive as the heavens themselves.
20: 'You who have made me experience many bitter troubles will revive me again; from the depths of the earth you will bring me up again' — the resurrection imagery: YHWH who sent the psalmist down to the 'depths of the earth' (Sheol) will bring him back up. This is not necessarily literal resurrection but the pattern of covenant rescue from near-death experiences: the one who went down comes back up. Yet the imagery reaches beyond the immediate situation to the pattern of divine life-from-death that early Judaism read as pointing to bodily resurrection (cf. Ezek 37's valley of dry bones).
21: 'You will increase my honor and surround me with comfort' — the reversal of the shame-cycle: the honor that the enemies sought to extinguish YHWH will 'increase.' Surrounded by comfort inverts the image of being surrounded by enemies. The same spatial encirclement that described threat (enemies conspiring, lying in wait) now describes safety (comfort encircling the psalmist).
22: 'I will praise you with the lyre for your faithfulness, O my God; I will sing to you with the harp, O Holy One of Israel' — the musical instruments of the temple worship: lyre and harp were the primary instruments of the Levitical singers (1 Chronicles 15:16; Psalms 25:1-6; Psalms 33:2; Psalms 150:3). 'Holy One of Israel' (qedosh yisrael) is the distinctive prophetic title for YHWH in Isaiah (appearing 26 times in Isaiah alone): the divine holiness that makes YHWH utterly unlike everything else. The psalmist's use of this Isaianic title suggests the influence of prophetic tradition on late psalmic composition.
23: 'My lips will shout for joy when I sing praises to you; and my soul, which you have redeemed, will also rejoice' — 'my soul which you have redeemed' — pada (redeem) is the ransom/release vocabulary of the covenant: YHWH paying the price to release the bound or enslaved (Deuteronomy 7:8: 'The LORD redeemed you from slavery'). The psalmist's soul has been specifically and personally redeemed by YHWH — an act of expensive covenant love, not merely general providential care.
24: 'And my tongue will speak of your righteous deeds all day long, for those who sought to harm me have been put to shame and disgraced' — the psalm closes with the same elements as it opened with (mouth, praise, shame of enemies) but now in the confident future tense or as completed action. The enemies' shame is the concluding vindication: the taunt of Psalms 71:10-11 ('God has abandoned him') has been answered by the reversal of their own position. The psalmist's ongoing speech throughout the day is both personal gratitude and public testimony that refutes the enemies' diagnosis.
